We are given that $\cos x + \cos y + \cos z = 1$ and $\sin x + \sin y + \sin z = 1.$

Squaring both equations, we get
\begin{align*}
(\cos x + \cos y + \cos z)^2 &= 1\\
\Rightarrow\qquad \cos^2 x + \cos^2 y + \cos^2 z + 2\cos x\cos y + 2\cos x\cos z + 2\cos y\cos z &= 1
\end{align*}and
\begin{align*}
(\sin x + \sin y + \sin z)^2 &= 1\\
\Rightarrow\qquad \sin^2 x + \sin^2 y + \sin^2 z + 2\sin x\sin y + 2\sin x\sin z + 2\sin y\sin z &= 1.
\end{align*}

We can use the identity $\cos^2 \theta + \sin^2 \theta = 1$ to simplify the equations above:
\begin{align*}
\cos^2 x + \cos^2 y + \cos^2 z + 2\cos x\cos y + 2\cos x\cos z + 2\cos y\cos z &= 1\\
\Rightarrow\qquad 3 + 2(\cos x\cos y + \cos x\cos z + \cos y\cos z) &= 1\\
\Rightarrow\qquad 2(\cos x\cos y + \cos x\cos z + \cos y\cos z) &= -2\\
\Rightarrow\qquad \cos x\cos y + \cos x\cos z + \cos y\cos z &= -1
\end{align*}and
\begin{align*}
\sin^2 x + \sin^2 y + \sin^2 z + 2\sin x\sin y + 2\sin x\sin z + 2\sin y\sin z &= 1\\
\Rightarrow\qquad 3 + 2(\sin x\sin y + \sin x\sin z + \sin y\sin z) &= 1\\
\Rightarrow\qquad 2(\sin x\sin y + \sin x\sin z + \sin y\sin z) &= -2\\
\Rightarrow\qquad \sin x\sin y + \sin x\sin z + \sin y\sin z &= -1.
\end{align*}

We can use the identity $\cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2 \theta - 1$ to find $\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z$:
\begin{align*}
\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z &= 2\cos^2 x - 1 + 2\cos^2 y - 1 + 2\cos^2 z - 1\\
&= 2(\cos^2 x + \cos^2 y + \cos^2 z) - 3\\
&= 2(1 - \sin^2 x + 1 - \sin^2 y + 1 - \sin^2 z) - 3\\
&= 2(3 - \sin^2 x - \sin^2 y - \sin^2 z) - 3\\
&= 6 - 2(\sin^2 x + \sin^2 y + \sin^2 z) - 3\\
&= 3 - 2(\sin^2 x + \sin^2 y + \sin^2 z).
\end{align*}

We can use the identity $\sin^2 \theta = \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$ to simplify the expression above:
\begin{align*}
\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z &= 3 - 2\left(\frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2} + \frac{1 - \cos 2y}{2} + \frac{1 - \cos 2z}{2}\right)\\
&= 3 - \left(1 - \frac{\cos 2x}{2} + 1 - \frac{\cos 2y}{2} + 1 - \frac{\cos 2z}{2}\right)\\
&= 3 - 3 + \frac{\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z}{2}\\
&= \frac{\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z}{2}.
\end{align*}

We can use the identity $\cos 2\theta = 2\cos^2 \theta - 1$ to find $\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z$:
\begin{align*}
\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z &= 2\cos^2 x - 1 + 2\cos^2 y - 1 + 2\cos^2 z - 1\\
&= 2(\cos^2 x + \cos^2 y + \cos^2 z) - 3\\
&= 2(1 - \sin^2 x + 1 - \sin^2 y + 1 - \sin^2 z) - 3\\
&= 2(3 - \sin^2 x - \sin^2 y - \sin^2 z) - 3\\
&= 6 - 2(\sin^2 x + \sin^2 y + \sin^2 z)\\
&= 3 - 2\left(\frac{1 - \cos 2x}{2} + \frac{1 - \cos 2y}{2} + \frac{1 - \cos 2z}{2}\right)\\
&= 3 - \left(1 - \frac{\cos 2x}{2} + 1 - \frac{\cos 2y}{2} + 1 - \frac{\cos 2z}{2}\right)\\
&= 3 - 3 + \frac{\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z}{2}\\
&= \frac{\cos 2x + \cos 2y + \cos 2z}{2}.
\end{align*}
